title: Midnight at the Moonlit Bakery: Mastering asyncio.gather
details: Story world: The user plays a sleepy apprentice wizard-baker at the Moonlit Bakery, a magical shop that only opens once the town's clocktower chimes dawn. Each night, the bakery receives multiple magical pastry orders (invisibility eclairs, luck croissants, dragon-scale bagels, etc.) that must be baked before sunrise. Baking each pastry is represented by a coroutine (uses asyncio.sleep to simulate bake time). Sequential baking is too slow, so the apprentice must learn to use asyncio.gather to bake multiple pastries concurrently, collect their results, handle burnt batches (exceptions), and dynamically manage a changing nightly order list. Learner is intermediate Python dev, already comfortable with async/await and coroutines; problem set is narrowly scoped to asyncio.gather mechanics only (no asyncio.create_task, TaskGroup, or other concurrency primitives unless needed for contrast). Tone: friendly, whimsical, lightly humorous baking/magic theme. Each part builds directly on functions/data from previous part (same bakery module, growing order list, same Pastry/BakeResult data shapes) so the whole set feels like one continuous night at the bakery.

parts.title: Part 1: Too Many Orders, Too Little Time
parts.details: Apprentice starts the night with 3-4 pastry orders that each take different simulated bake times (via asyncio.sleep). Baking sequentially with awaits in a loop is shown to be slow; goal is to rewrite using asyncio.gather to bake concurrently and collect all results in the same order as input, then compare total elapsed time vs sequential version.
parts.description_agent_notes:
- Introduce bakery premise briefly, keep light/funny
- Explain problem: apprentice awaits each pastry one by one, dawn is close, needs concurrency
- Explain asyncio.gather(*coroutines) runs coroutines concurrently and returns results list in same order as args, regardless of completion order
- Mention timing comparison (sequential vs gather) as a way to *see* concurrency benefit
- Keep explanation focused only on basic gather usage & ordering guarantee, no exceptions yet
parts.coding_agent_notes:
- Provide starter module bakery.py with: Pastry dataclass (name, bake_time), async def bake_pastry(pastry) -> str (returns e.g. f'{pastry.name} is ready!') using asyncio.sleep(pastry.bake_time)
- Provide list of 3-4 Pastry objects with varying bake_time (e.g. 1,2,1.5,2.5 seconds, keep short for testing)
- Skeleton function bake_all_sequential(pastries) fully implemented (loop+await) for comparison, given as reference/starter
- Skeleton function bake_all_concurrent(pastries) -> results list, TODO: use asyncio.gather to await all bake_pastry coroutines and return list of results in same order as input
- Provide a small timing harness (time.perf_counter) to print elapsed time for both versions, but leave gather implementation blank for learner
- Dataset: fixed list of pastries so results are deterministic for grading
- No exception-raising pastries in this part yet

parts.title: Part 2: The Burnt Batch Incident
parts.details: One of tonight's pastries is cursed and will always burn (raise an exception) when baked. Apprentice must learn to use asyncio.gather(*coroutines, return_exceptions=True) so a single failure doesn't crash the whole batch, and then inspect the results list to separate successful pastries from exceptions.
parts.description_agent_notes:
- Story: introduce a new order, 'Cursed Cinnamon Roll', that always burns and raises BakingError
- Explain default gather behavior: first exception raised propagates and cancels other awaited coroutines unless return_exceptions=True
- Explain return_exceptions=True causes gather to return exception objects inline in results list instead of raising
- Emphasize learner must then loop through results and use isinstance(result, Exception) to separate good pastries from burnt ones
- Keep focus strictly on this gather parameter and result inspection pattern
parts.coding_agent_notes:
- Extend bakery.py: add BakingError(Exception) class
- Add async def bake_pastry(pastry) modified (or new bake_cursed_pastry) that raises BakingError(f'{pastry.name} burned!') for a specific pastry named 'Cursed Cinnamon Roll' after its sleep
- Provide updated pastries list including this cursed one mixed with normal ones (reuse Part 1 style objects)
- Skeleton function bake_night_safe(pastries) -> tuple(successes: list[str], failures: list[str]): TODO use asyncio.gather(*[...], return_exceptions=True), then split results into successes (str results) and failures (str(exception) messages)
- Include a broken/naive version bake_night_unsafe(pastries) (already implemented without return_exceptions=True) that the learner can run first to observe the crash/cancellation behavior, contrasted with the TODO safe version
- Dataset: fixed list including exactly one cursed pastry and 3 normal ones for deterministic grading

parts.title: Part 3: The Ever-Changing Order Board
parts.details: The bakery's order board now receives a variable, unpredictable number of orders each night (list can be length 0 to N). Apprentice must build the list of bake coroutines dynamically (e.g. via list/generator comprehension) and pass them into asyncio.gather using unpacking (*), handling the edge case of zero orders gracefully.
parts.description_agent_notes:
- Story: the order board is enchanted and randomly changes size each night; sometimes no orders at all!
- Explain building a list of coroutine objects dynamically (comprehension) then unpacking with * into gather
- Explain edge case: asyncio.gather() with no arguments returns [] immediately, so empty order list should just short-circuit or return empty results without erroring
- Reinforce combination of return_exceptions=True from Part 2 with dynamic unpacking, since cursed pastries may or may not appear on the board
parts.coding_agent_notes:
- Extend bakery.py: add function build_bake_coroutines(pastries) -> list[coroutine objects] (calls bake_pastry(p) for each p, does NOT await, just creates coroutine objects)
- Skeleton function bake_dynamic_night(pastries) -> list[str|Exception-message]: TODO handle empty list case explicitly, then use asyncio.gather(*build_bake_coroutines(pastries), return_exceptions=True) and format results into strings (success messages or 'BURNT: <name>' style failure messages)
- Provide 3 test datasets: empty list, small list (2 pastries, no cursed), larger list (5-6 pastries with 1-2 cursed) for grading variety
- Reuse BakingError and cursed pastry logic from Part 2
- Keep function signatures consistent with Part 2 outputs where possible for continuity

parts.title: Part 4: The Dawn Bake-Off (Capstone)
parts.details: Final night before the big Dawn Bake-Off contest: apprentice must process a full, larger, randomly-sized order board (mixing normal and cursed pastries), concurrently bake everything with asyncio.gather, gracefully handle burnt batches, and produce a final night report summarizing successful pastries, burnt pastries, and total time taken versus a naive sequential baseline - tying together every gather skill learned.
parts.description_agent_notes:
- Story: big finale, town clocktower about to strike dawn, need one clean report of the night's results to present to the judges
- This is integrative: no new gather concepts, just combining Part 1-3 skills (basic gather, return_exceptions, dynamic unpacking) into one cohesive final function
- Emphasize producing a clear structured report (e.g. dict or dataclass) with counts/lists of successes and failures plus timing comparison
- Encourage confident, satisfying 'you did it' tone for wrap-up since this is the last part
parts.coding_agent_notes:
- Extend bakery.py with NightReport dataclass: successes list[str], failures list[str], elapsed_seconds float
- Provide reference sequential_bake_baseline(pastries) fully implemented (for timing comparison only, using naive loop+await, skipping cursed handling by catching exceptions manually)
- Skeleton function run_bake_off(pastries) -> NightReport: TODO must (1) build dynamic coroutine list, (2) call asyncio.gather with return_exceptions=True, (3) time the operation, (4) split results into successes/failures, (5) return populated NightReport
- Dataset: one larger randomized-but-fixed-seed order list (8-10 pastries, mix of normal + 2-3 cursed) for deterministic grading, reused from Part 3 pastry generator logic
- Grading should check NightReport fields exactly (order-insensitive comparison for lists acceptable) and confirm gather-based version completes faster than sequential baseline in elapsed_seconds
